straight_beat.name: Mickey Dougherty
straight_beat.column:
The Phillies lost again. That’s your scorecard. Not the kind you’d want on a report card, but the kind that stares you in the face after a 10-4 drubbing at Wrigley Field. You don’t need a crystal ball to know this isn’t the first time they’ve fallen flat in Chicago, but it’s the kind of loss that makes you wonder if the team’s been chasing shadows all season.  

The pitching staff, for all its promise, looked like a group of kids playing a grown-up game. Zach Eflin started strong, throwing six innings and allowing just two runs, but then the bullpen came unglued. They’ve been the story of this season, haven’t they? The hope that someone would step up, that a reliever would be the difference-maker. But here we are, with the same old script: a lead in the fifth, a meltdown in the sixth, and the Cubs laughing all the way to the dugout. The Phillies’ bullpen has been a roller coaster, and it’s starting to feel like a free ride for the opposition.  

And the offense? They’ve been a mixed bag. Some nights they’re flying high, other nights they’re stuck in a fog. Against the Cubs, they didn’t hit the ball hard, didn’t chase the pitch, and didn’t capitalize on the few opportunities they had. You can’t blame the bats for the lack of runs—there’s no magic bullet here. But

optimist.name: Terri 'Terry' Pascucci
optimist.column:
The mural on my wall—yes, that one, the one that’s been getting a little dusty since the last time the Phillies won a series—still stands. It’s got a bunch of Phillies players in it, all grinning like they’ve just won the World Series, even though they haven’t. The point is, every loss is just a setup for a bigger win. That’s the message. That’s the mural. That’s the cheesesteak stand.  

So, yeah, the Phillies got smoked yesterday, 10-4. The Cubs came in with a plan, and the Phillies? Well, they’re still figuring out theirs. But here’s the thing: you can’t win every game. You can

pessimist.name: Vinnie DePasquale
pessimist.column: Another loss, another step closer to the abyss. The Phillies blinked yesterday, letting the Cubs run roughshod over them like a bad dream you can’t wake up from. Ten to four? That’s not just a defeat—it’s a statement. A reminder that even when you’re supposed to be the team with the most reason to believe, you’re still
